did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning approach, specifically an optimized DenseNet121 convolutional neural network (CNN) with transfer learning, to classify skin lesions as benign or malignant using the HAM10000 and ISIC2020 datasets. The model achieved 87% accuracy in this dermatology task.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work using an optimized DenseNet121 CNN model to classify skin lesions from the HAM10000 and ISIC2020 datasets, reporting specific results such as 87% accuracy. Although published in conference proceedings (ICISS-2025), it contains a proposed methodology, experimental results, and discussion of new findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: CNN based Detection of Skin Lesions
journal: Proceedings of the 7th International Conference on Intelligent Sustainable Systems (ICISS-2025)
brief_summary: This study addresses the classification of skin lesions as benign or malignant to improve early detection of skin cancer. The authors propose an optimized DenseNet121 convolutional neural network model utilizing transfer learning and specific architectural enhancements such as ReLU and sigmoid activation layers. The model was trained and evaluated on a combined dataset of 3,297 images from the HAM10000 and ISIC2020 collections, incorporating preprocessing steps like data augmentation and hair excision. Experimental results demonstrated that the proposed method achieved an overall accuracy of 87% and an F-measure of 87%, outperforming existing approaches like SVM, EfficientNetB0, and VGG16. The findings indicate that the optimized DenseNet121 model is effective for skin cancer detection and holds promise for alleviating strain on healthcare diagnostic workflows.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as the malignant class in a binary classification task. The study utilized 1,497 malignant images from the combined HAM10000 and ISIC2020 datasets. The model achieved 85% accuracy (122/144) in identifying malignant lesions.

Used as the benign class in a binary classification task. The study utilized 1,800 benign images from the combined HAM10000 and ISIC2020 datasets. The model achieved 88% accuracy (163/186) in identifying benign lesions.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (benign vs. malignant skin lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly describes a binary classification task to discriminate between non-cancerous (benign) and cancerous (malignant) images. The model uses a sigmoid activation function in the output layer to generate probability values between 0 and 1, optimized with binary cross-entropy loss. The target classes are 'benign' and 'malignant' (melanoma), with the model achieving 87% overall accuracy, 88% accuracy for benign lesions, and 85% accuracy for malignant lesions.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 and ISIC2020 (Combined)
datasets_used.description: A combined dataset obtained from Kaggle's repository, aggregating the HAM10000 and ISIC2020 collections. It consists of dermoscopic images of skin lesions with diverse skin types, lesion sizes, shapes, colors, and textures.

use.training: Used for training the optimized DenseNet121 model with a training-testing split administered to appraise generalizability. Data augmentation (rotations, flipping, cropping) and preprocessing (hair excision, lighting adjustments, resizing to 224x224) were applied.
use.validation: Used for validation during the 50-epoch training process to monitor accuracy and loss convergence.
use.testing: Used for testing to evaluate model performance, resulting in an overall accuracy of 87% and an F-measure of 87%.
use.other: The dataset is noted as suitable for further research and creation of AI-based diagnostic platforms.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F-measure, and loss metrics without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000 and ISIC2020 (Combined).notes: The proposed optimized DenseNet121 model was trained for 50 epochs using Adam optimization with a mini-batch size of 15. It utilized transfer learning, data augmentation (rotations, flipping, cropping), hair excision, and lighting adjustments. The model achieved an overall accuracy of 87% and an F-measure of 87%. It correctly classified 88% of benign lesions (163 out of 186) and 85% of malignant lesions (122 out of 144). The study notes that this performance outperforms existing approaches such as SVM, EfficientNetB0, VGG16, Inception_v3, and Random Forest Classifier.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning (pre-trained on ImageNet, implied by standard DenseNet121 usage and context of transfer learning adoption)

enhancements: Two dense layers utilizing ReLU activation
Final output dense layer employing sigmoid activation function

preprocessing_techniques: Segmentation of lesions from surrounding skin
Standardization of dimensions, resolution, and color space

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to artificially expand and diversify the dataset to prevent overfitting. Specific probability or angle ranges were not reported.
Applied to artificially expand and diversify the dataset to prevent overfitting. Specific probability or axis were not reported.
Applied to artificially expand and diversify the dataset to prevent overfitting. Specific size or range were not reported.

conclusions: The optimized DenseNet121 convolutional neural network model achieved an overall accuracy of 87% and an F-measure of 87% in classifying skin lesions as benign or malignant using the combined HAM10000 and ISIC2020 datasets.
The proposed method outperformed existing approaches, including SVM, EfficientNetB0, VGG16, Inception_v3, and Random Forest Classifier.

The model demonstrated strong generalization capabilities, with training and validation accuracy curves converging and loss decreasing steadily over 50 epochs, indicating robustness and minimal overfitting.
Specifically, the model correctly classified 88% of benign lesions (163/186) and 85% of malignant lesions (122/144).
The study concludes that the proposed method holds promise for alleviating strain on healthcare systems and improving the efficiency of diagnostic workflows through optimized resource utilization.
The authors acknowledge that future research should focus on augmenting datasets with a varied and inclusive range of skin tones to reflect real-world diversity and enhance generalizability.
important_remarks: The study combines HAM10000 and ISIC2020 datasets into a single pool of 3,297 images (1,800 benign, 1,497 malignant) without specifying the exact split ratios for training, validation, and testing, raising potential data leakage or generalizability concerns.

The paper acknowledges that the dataset lacks diversity in skin tones and ethnicities, noting that future work must augment datasets with varied skin tones to reflect real-world diversity and enhance generalizability.
The paper references a 'ISIC2000' dataset in the abstract, which appears to be a typo for ISIC2020, indicating potential carelessness in documentation.

The model achieved 87% accuracy, which is lower than some state-of-the-art results cited in the literature review (e.g., 83.1% with MobileNet on HAM10000 alone in prior work), suggesting the combined dataset or preprocessing may have introduced noise or complexity.
No statistical significance testing (p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance claims are based solely on point estimates of accuracy and F-measure.

The preprocessing steps include 'hair excision' and 'segmentation of lesions from surrounding skin,' which may introduce bias if the segmentation algorithm is imperfect or if hair presence correlates with specific lesion types or skin tones.
The study is published in a conference proceeding (ICISS-2025) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may imply less rigorous scrutiny compared to journal publications.

The authors claim the model is suitable for 'clinical use' and 'real-world diagnostic applications,' but the lack of external validation on independent, diverse clinical datasets limits the validity of this claim.
